title: jit-project-lead skill: milestone-level vision steward above the epic lead
description:
Add a **`jit-project-lead`** skill: an autonomous steward operating one strategic tier above the current epic-level lead. It owns the project vision, plans and drives the top-tier container by delegating each sub-strategic container to a subordinate lead, resolves subordinate escalations against the vision, and enforces jit content standards across the whole project.

## Background

JIT's skill roster covers a single issue (`jit-manage`), the breakdown node (`jit-breakdown`), parallel execution (`jit-parallel`), planning (`jit-plan`, in progress), and one epic end-to-end (`project-lead`). The top strategic tier — the project vision and the container above the epic (the `milestone` tier in this repo's hierarchy) — has no owner. `project-lead` is single-container scope ("drive one epic, then stop") and routes every cross-container decision straight to the human.

jit is domain-agnostic: it backs this repo, the work in `../gf2`, and is intended for non-software domains such as research, where the strategic container is a `goal` rather than an `epic`. So this role derives its tiers, container types, and standards from the project's own configuration, never from hardcoded software semantics. "milestone" and "epic" below name *this* repo's hierarchy levels; the skill reads the equivalent strategic and sub-strategic types from `[type_hierarchy]`.

## Approach (settled design decisions)

- **Compositional, purely additive at the new tier.** `jit-project-lead` dispatches the existing epic lead as a subagent (one per sub-strategic container, in topological waves), the same way `project-lead` today dispatches workers. It never re-implements epic execution.
- **Rename for tier clarity.** Today's `project-lead` is renamed `jit-task-lead` (the lead that drives concrete work to completion); `jit-project-lead` is the new vision tier. Cross-references are swept.
- **Standing steward.** A persistent role, re-invoked across sessions, that owns a durable vision/charter artifact (vision plus a first-class decision log) and a resumable project-level progress file. Not single-shot.
- **Mode-aware front door.** Entry paths: (1) lead existing strategic work; (2) plan-and-execute a vague high-level goal (cold-start via `jit-plan` at the strategic altitude); (3) steering discussion (interactive vision and decision work, dispatching no workers); (4) project-wide content-standards sweep.
- **Absorb escalations; escalate up rarely.** The role resolves most subordinate escalations from the vision and decision log, and escalates to the human only for vision-level conflicts, cross-strategic-container dependencies, or project-wide infrastructure changes. The epic lead's escalation target becomes "whoever invoked it" (human when run standalone, parent lead when nested) — the single change to the existing skill.
- **Standards from a canonical home, not memory.** The scattered jit content standards (purpose-only descriptions, `[hard]`/`[aspirational]` markers, `REQ-NN` ids, standalone-readability, one-outcome-per-item, landable waves) are promoted into a versioned canonical doc the role consumes and enforces.

## Success Criteria

- [hard] REQ-01: A `jit-project-lead` skill exists with a thin orchestrator `SKILL.md` (within the agentskills budget: under 500 lines, roughly 5k tokens) and role prompts in `references/`. It discovers its strategic and sub-strategic tiers and container types from the project's `[type_hierarchy]`, hardcoding no domain-specific type names, so it works for software, `../gf2`, and research projects alike.
- [hard] REQ-02: The skill supports the four invocation modes — lead existing strategic work, plan-and-execute a high-level goal, steering discussion, and project-wide standards sweep — and routes an opening request to the correct path.
- [hard] REQ-03: The role owns a durable vision/charter artifact (vision plus a first-class decision log of chosen and rejected options with reasons) at a location read from the project's documentation config, and a resumable project-level progress file; both survive across sessions.
- [hard] REQ-04: The role drives sub-strategic containers by dispatching the renamed epic lead as a subagent, one per container in topological waves, and runs a cross-container coherence review before accepting each as complete.
- [hard] REQ-05: A parent-escalation policy resolves subordinate escalations against the vision and escalates to the human only for vision conflicts, cross-strategic-container dependencies, or project-wide infrastructure changes; the epic lead's escalation target is made context-aware (invoking lead versus human) without breaking its standalone use.
- [hard] REQ-06: The jit content standards are promoted into a single canonical versioned doc; both leads consume it, and the standards-sweep mode audits the project against it, auto-fixing mechanical violations and surfacing judgment ones.
- [hard] REQ-07: `project-lead` is renamed `jit-task-lead` with all cross-references swept (skills, evals, scripts, docs); the renamed and the new skill each activate from their descriptions and pass their evals.

## Non-goals

- Re-implementing epic execution — the epic lead remains the sole driver of an epic's bracket, breakdown, and implementation.
- Building `jit-plan` — that is task `eed6750c`; modes 2 and 3 consume it when it lands, and this work can ship mode 1 first.
- Baking in domain-specific tiers, gates, or standards — nothing software-specific is hardcoded.

## Open design questions (recorded, not resolved here)

- Rename target: `jit-task-lead` (chosen) versus `jit-epic-lead` (tier-matching) — the renamed lead drives epics, so the chosen name sits slightly below its actual tier.
- Whether to make the strategic container breakable in `.jit/templates.toml` (`applies_to`) so strategic-tier planning gets the same gated P-then-B bracket as epics, or keep strategic planning un-bracketed.
